title: Welcome to TigerBeetle
description: A guided tour of TigerBeetle — a high-performance financial transactions database built in Zig. Explore the consensus protocol, storage engine, accounting logic, client libraries, and testing infrastructure.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of databases and distributed systems

steps.title: The Big Picture
steps.description: TigerBeetle is a financial transactions database designed for mission-critical safety and extreme performance. The entire codebase is written in Zig — chosen for static memory allocation, deterministic execution, and fine-grained hardware control. The top-level layout is simple: `src/` holds all source code, `docs/` has documentation, and `build.zig` drives the build system.

interactiveActions.description: Hover over the src directory to see the core codebase

steps.title: Core Source Modules
steps.description: The `src/` directory contains everything: the database engine, consensus protocol, storage layer, client libraries, CLI, and tests. Key root-level files include `state_machine.zig` (the accounting engine), `constants.zig` (runtime configuration), `vopr.zig` (deterministic simulation tester), and `tigerbeetle.zig` (the main entry point). Each major subsystem lives in its own subdirectory.

interactiveActions.description: Click state_machine.zig — the heart of TigerBeetle's accounting logic

steps.title: VSR — Consensus Protocol
steps.description: TigerBeetle uses Viewstamped Replication (VSR) for distributed consensus across a 6-replica cluster. The `vsr/` directory implements the full protocol: `replica.zig` (565KB) is the core replica state machine handling replication, quorum consensus, and failover. `journal.zig` manages the write-ahead log, `grid.zig` provides the block storage abstraction (0.5 MiB blocks), and `superblock.zig` handles checkpoint metadata. The protocol uses flexible quorums — 3 replicas for durability, 4 for view change.

interactiveActions.description: Click replica.zig — the largest file in the repo and the core of consensus

steps.title: LSM — Storage Engine
steps.description: Data lives in a Log-Structured Merge Tree forest — multiple type-specialized trees optimized for different value sizes (128 bytes for transfers, 32 bytes for indices). `forest.zig` orchestrates the multi-tree structure, `tree.zig` implements individual trees, and `compaction.zig` runs deterministic background compaction to bound tail latency. `manifest.zig` tracks tree versions, while `scan_*.zig` files handle query iteration. All compaction is spread deterministically so no single operation pays the full cost.

interactiveActions.description: Click forest.zig to see how multiple LSM trees are orchestrated

steps.title: State Machine — Accounting Logic
steps.description: The `state_machine/` directory and `state_machine.zig` implement TigerBeetle's double-entry bookkeeping engine. Operations include `create_accounts`, `create_transfers`, `lookup_accounts`, and `lookup_transfers`, with support for pending, posted, and voided transfer states. `workload.zig` generates deterministic test workloads, while `auditor.zig` validates that accounting invariants always hold. The state machine is physically deterministic — the same input produces byte-for-byte identical state across all replicas.

interactiveActions.description: Hover over the state_machine directory to see the workload and auditor modules

steps.title: Client Libraries — 7 Languages
steps.description: TigerBeetle provides client libraries for Go, Python, Rust, Java, .NET, Node.js, and C. All clients follow the same pattern: a Zig-compiled shared library (`libtb_client`) wrapped with language-specific bindings (FFI, JNI, cgo, ctypes, P/Invoke, etc.). This means every client gets the same battle-tested networking and batching code. The common API exposes `create_accounts`, `create_transfers`, and lookup operations with async batching.

interactiveActions.description: Explore the client directories — each follows the same shared-library wrapper pattern

steps.title: I/O Layer — Platform Abstraction
steps.description: The `io/` directory abstracts platform-specific I/O. On Linux (production), TigerBeetle uses io_uring for high-throughput async I/O. macOS uses kqueue, Windows uses IOCP. `common.zig` provides shared abstractions, and `test.zig` is a mock backend for deterministic testing. This design allows the entire database to be tested in simulation without touching real I/O.

interactiveActions.description: Click linux.zig to see the io_uring production I/O implementation

steps.title: Testing — Simulation & Fuzzing
steps.description: TigerBeetle's testing is extraordinary. The VOPR (Viewstamped Operation Replicator) in `vopr.zig` runs an entire cluster on a single thread with deterministic simulation — injecting storage faults, network partitions, and timing variations. The `testing/` directory provides `cluster.zig` for in-process cluster simulation, `packet_simulator.zig` for network fault injection, and `storage.zig` for simulated storage failures. There are 18 fuzz test modules throughout the codebase. The Vortex framework in `testing/vortex/` drives full end-to-end integration tests.

interactiveActions.description: Click cluster.zig — the in-process cluster simulator used for deterministic testing

steps.title: Documentation & Philosophy
steps.description: The `docs/` directory contains user-facing guides, API reference, and deep-dive internals. `TIGER_STYLE.md` is the project's coding philosophy — emphasizing safety, performance, zero technical debt, and static allocation. The `internals/` subdirectory has detailed architecture documents for VSR, LSM, data formats, upgrades, and synchronization. `start.md` is the getting-started guide for new users.

interactiveActions.description: Click TIGER_STYLE.md — the project's coding philosophy and design principles

steps.title: The Complete Architecture
steps.description: You've now seen TigerBeetle's major subsystems: VSR consensus (blue), LSM storage (purple), the accounting state machine (amber), client libraries (cyan), platform I/O (orange), and the testing infrastructure (red). The architecture is built on key principles: determinism (same input = same physical state), static memory allocation (no malloc after startup), single-threaded execution for the state machine, aggressive batching (8,000 transfers per prepare), and mechanical sympathy (direct I/O, cache-line alignment, SIMD). This is a database engineered from the ground up for financial transaction safety and performance.

interactiveActions.description: Freely explore the full codebase — hover over any directory or click any file to learn more
